title: Soft amaretti
subtitle: Italian almond biscuits, chewy in the centre and crackled outside
excerpt: Soft amaretti are made from ground almonds, sugar, and egg whites with no flour. They crackle on the outside as they bake and stay chewy inside. Naturally gluten-free.
sourceNotes: Amaretti (from the Italian amaro, bitter, referring to the bitter almond flavour) have been made in northern Italy since at least the eighteenth century, with the Saronno region associated with the dried, crunchy type. The soft variety (amaretti morbidi) is a regional variant common in Lombardy and Piedmont; the base of ground almonds, sugar, and egg whites appears consistently in Italian pasticceria manuals from the nineteenth century onward.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keep in an airtight tin for up to a week. The chewy centre firms slightly over time but the biscuits stay good.

content.text: There is no flour here: the structure comes entirely from ground almonds and egg whites. The mixture is wetter than most biscuit doughs; rolling the balls in icing sugar before baking gives the characteristic crackled surface. Don't be tempted to add flour if the dough seems sticky; wet hands are all that is needed to roll the balls. Bake until the cracks are golden and the biscuits have puffed slightly; the centres will still feel soft when pressed but firm up as they cool.

Heat the oven to 170°C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Combine the ground almonds and caster sugar in a large bowl. Add the egg whites, almond extract, and vanilla. Mix until the dough comes together; it will be sticky. Pour the icing sugar onto a plate. With damp hands, roll the dough into balls about 2.5 cm across (roughly the size of a large cherry). Roll each ball in the icing sugar to coat thickly and place on the prepared trays, spaced 4 cm apart.

Bake for 16 to 18 minutes, until the biscuits have puffed, cracked on the surface, and are golden at the cracks. The centres will feel soft when pressed; this is correct. Leave on the trays for 5 minutes before moving to a rack. They firm up to a chewy, springy texture as they cool completely.
